title: Bosonic-Fermionic Diagonal Coinvariants and Theta Operators
overall_summary: This paper is primarily a conjectural and expository note connecting multivariate diagonal coinvariant modules with bosonic and fermionic variable sets, Macdonald Delta and Theta operators, and combinatorial models such as parking functions, multilabeled Dyck paths, and tiered trees. Its most useful structural contribution for the original problem is the stable GL_k √ó GL_j √ó S_n character framework: the Schur expansion in grading variables is independent of the number of bosonic or fermionic variable sets once enough variables are present, and the Hilbert series is obtained from the Frobenius characteristic by pairing with p_1^n. The paper formulates the central boson-fermion plethystic conjecture C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q‚àíŒµu;z), where E_n is the pure bosonic stable Frobenius characteristic; this is especially relevant to hook Schur coefficients because the GL(1|1) specialization q‚àíŒµu is supported on hook shapes. It also records a one-boson/one-fermion standard-tableau formula involving descents, major index, and q-binomial coefficients, which is a plausible route to a combinatorial interpretation of hook coefficients after extracting ordinary hook Schur terms. Further potentially useful material includes the skew-operator link between E_n and Delta operators, and t=1 combinatorial expansions of super nabla operators by multilabeled Dyck paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Character framework for coefficient-stable G-modules, Equations 3.1-3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B=(Œ≤_ab) be a k by n matrix of commuting bosonic variables, let F=(œÜ_cd) be a j by n matrix of anticommuting fermionic variables, and let R_{k,j;n}=Q[B;F]. The group G=GL_k √ó GL_j √ó S_n acts by f(B;F) ‚Ü¶ f(PBœÉ;QFœÉ), where P‚ààGL_k, Q‚ààGL_j, and œÉ is an n by n permutation matrix acting diagonally on columns. If V is a G-stable submodule or stable quotient of R_{k,j;n}, then its multigraded Frobenius characteristic has the form V(q;u;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n of (sum over Œª,œÅ of v^{ŒªœÅ}_Œº s_Œª(q)s_œÅ(u)) s_Œº(z), where q=(q_1,...,q_k), u=(u_1,...,u_j), s_Œª and s_œÅ are GL characters, and s_Œº(z) is the Frobenius image of the irreducible S_n-module indexed by Œº. The multigraded Hilbert series, or GL_k √ó GL_j character obtained by forgetting the S_n action, is V(q;u)=‚ü®V(q;u;z),p_1(z)^n‚ü©=sum over Œº‚ä¢n of (sum over Œª,œÅ of v^{ŒªœÅ}_Œº s_Œª(q)s_œÅ(u)) f^Œº, where f^Œº=‚ü®s_Œº,p_1^n‚ü© is the number of standard tableaux of shape Œº. For the diagonal coinvariant modules C_{k,j;n}, the paper writes C_n(q;u;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n c_Œº(q;u)s_Œº(z), with c_Œº(q;u)=sum over Œª,œÅ c^{ŒªœÅ}_Œº s_Œª(q)s_œÅ(u), and emphasizes that these coefficients are stable, i.e. independent of k and j once enough variables are present.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard character decomposition for commuting actions. Because the actions of GL_k, GL_j, and S_n commute, and the relevant finite multihomogeneous pieces are semisimple, V decomposes into irreducibles of the form S_Œª(C^k) ‚äó S_œÅ(C^j) ‚äó S^Œº with multiplicities v^{ŒªœÅ}_Œº. The characters of S_Œª(C^k) and S_œÅ(C^j) are Schur polynomials s_Œª(q) and s_œÅ(u), while the Frobenius characteristic sends S^Œº to s_Œº(z). Pairing with p_1(z)^n replaces each irreducible S_n-character by its dimension, since ‚ü®s_Œº,p_1^n‚ü©=f^Œº. Coefficient stability is taken as part of the setup for the modules considered: when k or j is too small, Schur functions with too many parts vanish, but the stable multiplicities themselves do not depend on k,j.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Haiman theorem for two bosonic sets, Equation 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_{2;n}=C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n]/I_n(x,y), where I_n(x,y) is generated by all nonconstant S_n-invariant polynomials under the diagonal action permuting x_i and y_i simultaneously. Let C_{2;n}(q,t;z) be its bigraded Frobenius characteristic, with q tracking x-degree and t tracking y-degree. Let ‚àá be the Bergeron-Garsia nabla operator on modified Macdonald polynomials, defined by ‚àá HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;z)=T_Œº HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;z), where T_Œº=product over cells (i,j) in Œº of q^i t^j, using the paper's cell coordinates. Then Haiman's theorem states that C_{2;n}(q,t;z)=‚àá e_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Haiman's result and does not reproduce the proof. The proof in the cited work uses Hilbert schemes, the n! theorem, and the geometry of the isospectral Hilbert scheme to identify the bigraded Frobenius characteristic of diagonal coinvariants with the Macdonald-eigenoperator expression ‚àáe_n. In the present paper this theorem is used as the two-bosonic-set specialization of the general stable bosonic character E_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Œ©(q)=product over i=1,...,k of (1‚àíq_i)^{-1}=sum_{r‚â•0} h_r(q) and Œ©'(u)=product over i=1,...,j of (1+u_i)=sum_{r‚â•0} e_r(u), the Frobenius characteristic of the full boson-fermion polynomial ring R_n=R_{k,j;n}=Q[B;F] is R_n(q;u;z)=h_n[Œ©(q) Œ©'(u) z] in plethystic notation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each column indexed by 1,...,n, the commuting variables contribute the symmetric algebra character Œ©(q), while the anticommuting variables contribute the exterior algebra character Œ©'(u). The symmetric group S_n permutes the n columns. The Frobenius characteristic of an n-fold set of such weighted letters is obtained by the cycle-index/plethystic construction h_n[A z], with A=Œ©(q)Œ©'(u). This gives h_n[Œ©(q)Œ©'(u)z]. The paper states this proposition without a detailed proof, relying on standard plethystic character calculus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Plethystic identity for bosonic and fermionic alphabets, Equation 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's plethystic notation, Œµ is defined by p_i[Œµ]=(-1)^i, so that p_i[‚àíŒµ u]=œâ p_i(u). The identity Œ©[q‚àíŒµu]=sum over n‚â•0 and k+j=n of h_k(q)e_j(u)=Œ©(q)Œ©'(u) holds. More generally, the skew Cauchy expansion gives s_Œª[q‚àíŒµu]=sum over ŒΩ‚äÜŒª of s_ŒΩ(q)s_{Œª'/ŒΩ'}(u), where Œª' and ŒΩ' are conjugate partitions and s_{Œª'/ŒΩ'} is a skew Schur function.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first identity is the plethystic translation of multiplying the generating series for complete symmetric functions in bosonic variables by the generating series for elementary symmetric functions in fermionic variables. The sign alphabet ‚àíŒµu converts complete symmetric functions into elementary symmetric functions via the involution œâ. The Schur expansion follows from the standard skew Cauchy identity applied to the difference of alphabets q‚àíŒµu. This identity is explicitly recalled in the paper and is the algebraic mechanism behind the boson-fermion substitution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the purely bosonic polynomial ring case j=0, let r_Œº(q)=‚ü®R_n(q;0;z),s_Œº(z)‚ü© be the coefficient of s_Œº(z) in the Frobenius characteristic of the full polynomial ring R_{k,0;n}. Then r_Œº(q)=s_Œº[Œ©(q)]=s_Œº[sum_{i‚â•0} h_i(q)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set u=0 in Proposition 1 to get R_n(q;0;z)=h_n[Œ©(q)z]. The Cauchy identity in plethystic form expands h_n[Xz] as sum over Œº‚ä¢n of s_Œº[X]s_Œº(z). Taking X=Œ©(q), the coefficient of s_Œº(z) is s_Œº[Œ©(q)]. The paper notes that the Schur expansion of this expression is connected with the restriction problem for GL_k characters restricted to permutation matrices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Stable pure bosonic expansion cited from Bergeron, Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E_n denote the stable Frobenius characteristic of the pure bosonic multivariate diagonal coinvariant module. The paper recalls that there is a coefficient-stable expression E_n=sum over Œº‚ä¢n of a_Œº ‚äó s_Œº, where a_Œº=sum over Œª of a^Œª_Œº s_Œª, equivalently E_n(q;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n (sum over Œª a^Œª_Œº s_Œª(q)) s_Œº(z). The integers a^Œª_Œº vanish unless Œª has size at most binom(n,2)‚àíŒ∑(Œº') and has at most n‚àíŒº_1 parts, where Œ∑(Œ±)=sum_i Œ±_i(i‚àí1) and Œº' is the conjugate partition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this stable expression from Bergeron's work and does not prove it. The underlying idea is that the pure bosonic diagonal coinvariant module carries commuting GL_k and S_n actions, so its stable character decomposes into Schur functors for GL_k and irreducibles for S_n. The stated degree and length bounds are imported from the cited theory of multivariate diagonal harmonics and ensure that only finitely many Schur functions s_Œª(q) occur for each Œº.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 2, Boson-Fermion Frobenius
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C_n(q;u;z) be the stable Frobenius characteristic of the boson-fermion diagonal coinvariant module C_{k,j;n}, and let E_n(q;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n a_Œº(q)s_Œº(z) be the stable pure bosonic Frobenius characteristic. The conjecture states that C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q‚àíŒµu;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n a_Œº[q‚àíŒµu]s_Œº(z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n sum over Œª a^Œª_Œº s_Œª[q‚àíŒµu]s_Œº(z). Using the skew expansion, this is equivalently C_n=sum over Œº‚ä¢n sum over Œª a^Œª_Œº (sum over ŒΩ‚äÜŒª s_ŒΩ ‚äó s_{Œª'/ŒΩ'}) ‚äó s_Œº. The paper lists several specializations: with no fermions, C_n(q_1,...,q_k;0;z)=E_n(q_1,...,q_k;z); with two bosonic variables, C_n(q+t;0;z)=‚àáe_n(z); with one fermionic variable and no bosons, C_n(0;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n‚àí1} u^a s_{(n‚àía,1^a)}(z); with several fermionic variables and no bosons, C_n(0;u_1,...,u_j;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n (sum over Œª a^Œª_Œº s_{Œª'}(u_1,...,u_j))s_Œº(z); and for a single bosonic variable q, C_n(q;0;z)=hat h_n(z)/hat h_n(1), where hat f(z)=f[z/(1‚àíq)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a conjecture; the paper gives no proof. The evidence described is compatibility with known and conjectured special cases, including Haiman's two-boson theorem, the pure fermionic specializations, and formulas involving Delta and Theta operators. Section 6 explains one mechanism behind the conjecture: the identity sum_a u^a e_a^‚ä• f(q)=f[q‚àíŒµu] and the conjectural Delta-via-skew formula would imply the one-fermion, two-boson specialization C_n(q+t;u;z)=E_n[q+t‚àíŒµu;z].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: One-boson/one-fermion standard-tableau formula, Equation 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For one bosonic variable q and one fermionic variable u, N. Bergeron, Machacek, and Zabrocki conjectured that C_n(q;u;z)=sum_{r=0}^{n‚àí1} sum over Œª‚ä¢n sum over œÑ‚ààSYT(Œª) of q^{Œ±_r(œÑ)} [des(œÑ) choose r]_q u^r s_Œª(z), where des(œÑ) is the number of descents of the standard tableau œÑ, maj(œÑ) is its major index, Œ±_r(œÑ)=maj(œÑ)‚àír des(œÑ)+binom(r,2), and [des(œÑ) choose r]_q is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, interpreted as zero if r>des(œÑ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a conjecture and does not prove it. A footnote states that it follows from Zabrocki's conjectural formula C_n(q+t;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n‚àí1} u^a Œî'_{e_{n‚àía‚àí1}}e_n(z) together with a formula of Haglund, Rhoades, and Shimozono. The paper also rewrites it as C_n(q;u;z)=sum_{a=0}^{n‚àí1} u^a Œî'_{e_{n‚àía‚àí1}}e_n(z) evaluated at t=0, again conditional on the conjectural Delta-coinvariant connection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Kim-Rhoades pure fermionic two-variable formula, Equations 5.3-5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For two fermionic variables u and v and no bosonic variables, Kim and Rhoades prove the formula C_n(0;u+v;z)=sum over a,b‚â•0 with a+b‚â§n‚àí1 of u^a v^b (s_{(n‚àía,1^a)} ‚ãÜ s_{(n‚àíb,1^b)} ‚àí s_{(n‚àía+1,1^{a‚àí1})} ‚ãÜ s_{(n‚àíb+1,1^{b‚àí1})})(z), with the convention that invalid hook partitions contribute zero. Here ‚ãÜ is the Kronecker product of Schur functions. Equivalently, using Frobenius notation (a|b)=(a+1,1^b) and Kronecker coefficients g^Œº_{Œ±,Œ≤}=‚ü®s_Œ±‚ãÜs_Œ≤,s_Œº‚ü©, the coefficient of s_Œº(z) is a sum of differences g^Œº_{(a|b),(c|d)}‚àíg^Œº_{(a+1|b‚àí1),(c+1|d‚àí1)} over b+d‚â§n‚àí1 with a+b=c+d=n‚àí1. The paper notes that these differences are known to be positive, citing Remmel.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Kim and Rhoades for the proof and does not reproduce it. The cited result comes from Lefschetz theory for exterior algebras and fermionic diagonal coinvariants. The reformulation in terms of Kronecker coefficients is obtained by expanding each Kronecker product in the Schur basis and collecting the coefficient of s_Œº(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Set all bosonic parameters q_i equal to 1 for i=1,...,k and all fermionic parameters u_i equal to 1 for i=1,...,j, and write C_n(k;j;z) for the resulting Frobenius characteristic. Then the coefficient of each s_Œº(z) in the Schur expansion of C_n(k;j;z) is a polynomial in k and j with rational coefficients. Consequently, the total dimension C_n(k;j), obtained by replacing each s_Œº(z) by f^Œº, is also a polynomial in k and j with rational coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using coefficient stability, the coefficient of s_Œº(z) is a finite sum of stable multiplicities times specializations s_Œª(1^k)s_œÅ(1^j). Each specialization s_Œª(1^k) is a polynomial in k with rational coefficients by the hook-content formula, and similarly s_œÅ(1^j) is polynomial in j. Finite sums and the final dimension-weighted sum by f^Œº preserve polynomiality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conjecture 3, Delta via skew
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every a, the paper recalls the conjecture ((e_a^‚ä• ‚äó Id)E_n)(q+t;z)=sum over Œº‚ä¢n (e_a^‚ä• a_Œº)(q+t)s_Œº(z)=Œî'_{e_{n‚àía‚àí1}}(e_n(z)). Here e_a^‚ä• is the Hall-inner-product adjoint to multiplication by e_a, E_n=sum_Œº a_Œº‚äós_Œº is the stable pure bosonic Frobenius characteristic, q+t means evaluation of the GL-character part in two variables q,t, and Œî'_f is the Macdonald Delta operator defined by Œî'_f HÃÉ_Œº=f[B_Œº(q,t)‚àí1]HÃÉ_Œº with B_Œº(q,t)=sum over cells (i,j) in Œº of q^i t^j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a conjecture from the cited GL_k √ó S_n diagonal harmonics work, not proved in this paper. The paper explains its role: for any symmetric function f(q), sum_a u^a e_a^‚ä• f(q)=f[q‚àíŒµu]. Therefore Conjecture 3 would transform the stable bosonic character into the one-fermion specialization and yield C_n(q+t;u;z)=E_n[q+t‚àíŒµu;z], matching Zabrocki's Delta-operator conjecture.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Hook component identity for the super nabla operator, Section 8.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ‚àá_v be the super nabla operator defined on modified Macdonald polynomials by ‚àá_v HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;z)=HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;v)HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;z). The paper recalls the well-known identity ‚ü®HÃÉ_Œº(q,t;v),s_{n‚àík,1^k}(v)‚ü©=e_k[B_Œº(q,t)‚àí1] for 0‚â§k‚â§n‚àí1. Consequently, the coefficient of the hook Schur function s_{n‚àík,1^k}(v) in ‚àá_v e_n(z) is Œî'_{e_k}e_n(z). At t=1, under the multilabeled path model for ‚àá_v e_n, extracting this hook component corresponds to restricting to paths whose reading word in the v-labels is a shuffle of the increasing sequence k+1,k+2,...,n and the decreasing sequence k,k‚àí1,...,1; the paper explains that such paths map naturally to valley-decorated labeled Dyck paths.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Macdonald hook scalar-product formula gives the coefficient of s_{n‚àík,1^k}(v) in each HÃÉ_Œº(v). Applying it termwise in the Macdonald expansion of ‚àá_v e_n(z) produces exactly the eigenvalue e_k[B_Œº‚àí1], which is the defining eigenvalue of Œî'_{e_k}; hence the hook coefficient is Œî'_{e_k}e_n. The t=1 path restriction uses a standard construction from the shuffle-theorem literature: Schur-hook extraction is encoded by reading words that are shuffles of the specified increasing and decreasing sequences. The nonascent condition in the multilabeled Dyck path then forces the corresponding rows to be movable valleys.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3, t=1 multilabeled Dyck path formula for super nabla
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers n and k, let MD_{kn} be the set of multilabeled kn-Dyck paths defined as follows. A path D goes from (0,0) to (kn,n), uses North and East steps, stays weakly above the relevant diagonal, and has area equal to the number of complete cells between D and the diagonal. A multilabeling assigns to the i-th North step a word w_i=(w_i^1,...,w_i^{k+1}) of positive integers, subject to the condition that the number of East steps of D along row y=i is at least the number of components j for which w_i^j‚â•w_{i+1}^j. For P=(D,w), define X^P=product over i=1,...,n of v_{1,w_i^1}‚ãØv_{k,w_i^k} z_{w_i^{k+1}}. Then the t=1 specialization of iterated super nabla is ‚àá_{v_1}‚ãØ‚àá_{v_k} e_n(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P‚ààMD_{kn} of q^{area(P)} X^P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proposition is quoted from Bergeron-Haglund-Iraci-Romero and is not proved in this paper. Conceptually, at t=1 the super nabla operator admits a monomial expansion indexed by Dyck paths with multiple label sets; the q-power records the area statistic, the labels record the monomial in the v_i and z alphabets, and the row-wise nonascent condition is precisely the compatibility condition ensuring the labels define an allowed multilabeled path.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5, Schur expansion of t=1 multilabeled path formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A labeling w=w_1‚ãØw_n of a multilabeled path is called lattice if, for every component j, the sequence (w_1^j,w_2^j,...,w_n^j) is a lattice word. For a lattice labeling, let Œª_j(P) be the partition associated with the j-th component lattice word. Let LMD_{kn} be the set of lattice multilabeled kn-Dyck paths. Then ‚àá_{v_1}‚ãØ‚àá_{v_k}e_n(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P‚ààLMD_{kn} of q^{area(P)} s_{Œª_1(P)}(v_1)‚ãØs_{Œª_k(P)}(v_k)s_{Œª_{k+1}(P)}(z). More generally, for a nonempty partition Œ≥, the analogous marked Œ≥,kn-Dyck paths RLMD_{Œ≥,kn} give Œî_{m_Œ≥}‚àá_{v_1}‚ãØ‚àá_{v_k}((-1)^{n‚àí1}p_n(z)) evaluated at t=1 as the corresponding sum over P‚ààRLMD_{Œ≥,kn} of q^{area(P)} s_{Œª_1(P)}(v_1)‚ãØs_{Œª_k(P)}(v_k)s_{Œª_{k+1}(P)}(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proposition is quoted from Bergeron-Haglund-Iraci-Romero. It is obtained from the monomial formula by applying the standard highest-weight or lattice-word extraction rule for Schur expansions: in a symmetric function, the coefficient of s_Œª can be read from monomials whose words are lattice words of shape Œª. Applying this independently in each variable set v_1,...,v_k,z converts the monomial sum over all multilabeled paths into a simultaneous Schur-positive sum over lattice multilabeled paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Xi-operator identities, Section 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít), let f^*(z)=f[z/M], and let ŒÝ be the Macdonald eigenoperator with eigenvalue product over nonzero cells (i,j) of Œº of (1‚àíq^i t^j) on HÃÉ_Œº. The paper defines ‚àá_M=MŒî_{e_1}ŒÝ and ŒûF(z)=‚àá_M F^*(z). Using Macdonald orthogonality, it obtains Œûe_Œª(z)=‚ü®‚àá_v e_n(z),h_Œª(v)‚ü© and equivalently ‚àá_v e_n(z)=sum over Œª‚ä¢n of m_Œª(v) Œûe_Œª(z). At t=1, if MD^1_{1n}(Œª) denotes the set of one-v-label Dyck paths whose v-label multiset has content Œª, then Œûe_Œª(z) evaluated at t=1 equals sum over P‚ààMD^1_{1n}(Œª) of q^{area(P)} e_{Œ∑(P)}(z), where Œ∑(P) is the composition extracted from the path by subtracting label nonascents from horizontal run lengths as defined in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper starts from the Macdonald expansion e_Œª^*=sum_Œº HÃÉ_Œº/w_Œº ¬∑ ‚ü®HÃÉ_Œº,e_Œª^*‚ü©_* and uses the relation ‚ü®HÃÉ_Œº,e_Œª^*‚ü©_*=‚ü®HÃÉ_Œº,h_Œª‚ü©. Applying ‚àá_M and comparing with the definition of super nabla gives Œûe_Œª=‚ü®‚àá_v e_n,h_Œª(v)‚ü©. Expanding in the monomial basis in the v variables gives ‚àá_v e_n=sum_Œª m_Œª(v)Œûe_Œª. The t=1 path formula follows by specializing the multilabeled Dyck path model and grouping the z-label contributions into elementary symmetric functions indexed by Œ∑(P).

proof_techniques.name: Stable GL-character packaging of multigraded Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats diagonal coinvariant spaces as modules for GL_k √ó GL_j √ó S_n, with GL_k acting on bosonic variable sets and GL_j on fermionic variable sets. This upgrades a multigraded Hilbert series into a Schur expansion in grading alphabets q and u, together with an S_n Frobenius characteristic in z. The Hilbert series is then recovered by pairing the z-Frobenius characteristic with p_1^n, which simply weights each S_n-isotypic coefficient by the dimension f^Œº of the corresponding irreducible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original pure bosonic algebra R_n^{(m)}, this says that the universal Hilbert coefficient c_Œª(n) is obtained from the stable pure bosonic Frobenius coefficients by c_Œª(n)=sum_Œº f^Œº a^Œª_Œº. Thus one can first seek a combinatorial interpretation of the refined coefficients a^Œª_Œº, or use the p_1^n pairing to turn tableau formulas in Œº into Hilbert-series formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Supersymmetric specialization q‚àíŒµu to access hook Schur coefficients
proof_techniques.description: The central plethystic device is replacing the bosonic alphabet q by the virtual alphabet q‚àíŒµu, with s_Œª[q‚àíŒµu]=sum_{ŒΩ‚äÜŒª}s_ŒΩ(q)s_{Œª'/ŒΩ'}(u). This transforms a pure bosonic stable character into a conjectural boson-fermion character. In the especially important GL(1|1) case, where q and u are single variables, this specialization is supported exactly on partitions fitting the (1,1)-hook, i.e. ordinary hook shapes. An immediate consequence of the displayed skew formula is that for Œª=(r,1^s), one has s_Œª[q‚àíŒµu]=q^r u^s+q^{r‚àí1}u^{s+1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's most direct route to hook coefficients: compare the q^a u^b monomial coefficients in the one-boson/one-fermion character with the two-term expansions of hook Schur functions. Combined with the tableau formula in Equation 5.2 and the Hilbert pairing with p_1^n, this suggests an explicit inversion giving c_{(r,1^s)}(n) from standard-tableau or permutation statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Skewing by e_a^‚ä• and Delta-operator extraction
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes the identity sum_a u^a e_a^‚ä• f(q)=f[q‚àíŒµu]. This means that the fermionic variable u can be interpreted as recording the effect of skewing the bosonic Schur expansion by elementary symmetric functions. Conjecture 3 identifies the evaluation of these skewed pieces at q+t with Macdonald Delta operators: (e_a^‚ä•‚äóId)E_n evaluated at q+t is conjecturally Œî'_{e_{n‚àía‚àí1}}e_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions are controlled by adding or removing vertical strips, exactly the operation governed by e_a^‚ä•. A proof strategy for hook coefficients could translate the desired hook coefficient into a finite difference of Delta-specialized coefficients, then use known Delta formulas at t=0 or t=1, or parking-function models, to give a count.

proof_techniques.name: Standard-tableau q-binomial expansion for the GL(1|1) specialization
proof_techniques.description: The one-boson/one-fermion formula expresses C_n(q;u;z) as a sum over standard tableaux œÑ, with weight q^{maj(œÑ)‚àír des(œÑ)+binom(r,2)} [des(œÑ) choose r]_q u^r. The Gaussian binomial coefficient can be interpreted combinatorially as choosing a subset or subpartition within a des(œÑ)-by-r rectangle, depending on the desired model. This formula packages the fermionic degree r and the bosonic degree into classical descent and major-index statistics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After extracting hook Schur terms through the GL(1|1) specialization and then applying the Hilbert pairing, this formula should convert hook coefficients c_Œª(n) into counts of tableaux, or via RSK into counts of permutations, with descent-set choices and major-index shifts.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald hook scalar product
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the identity ‚ü®HÃÉ_Œº,s_{n‚àík,1^k}‚ü©=e_k[B_Œº‚àí1] to identify hook Schur coefficients in the super-nabla variable set with Delta operators. This is a powerful way to replace a hook-extraction problem by an operator eigenvalue problem on the modified Macdonald basis.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original hook coefficient problem can be embedded into a super-nabla or Delta-operator specialization, this identity gives a canonical hook-extraction mechanism and connects the coefficient to the Delta Conjecture combinatorics.

proof_techniques.name: Highest-weight or lattice-word extraction of Schur coefficients from path monomials
proof_techniques.description: At t=1, the paper has monomial expansions of super nabla operators over multilabeled Dyck paths. It then converts these monomial sums into Schur-positive expansions by retaining only lattice labelings, where each component word is a lattice word and hence determines a partition Œª_j(P). This is the standard crystal/highest-weight method for reading Schur coefficients from a symmetric monomial generating function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A path model for the Hilbert coefficients c_Œª(n) could be obtained by imposing that the grading-label word has hook shape Œª, or by using the GL(1|1) specialization where only hook highest weights survive. This may yield a direct Dyck-path interpretation for hook coefficients in specializations such as t=1.

proof_techniques.name: Fermionic hook-Kronecker reduction
proof_techniques.description: The pure two-fermion specialization is expressed by Kim and Rhoades as positive differences of Kronecker products of hook Schur functions. This translates fermionic diagonal coinvariant coefficients into known hook-Kronecker coefficient formulas, for which Remmel-type combinatorics exists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Via the boson-fermion conjecture, pure bosonic Schur coefficients are conjugated into pure fermionic coefficients. For hook or near-hook shapes, known combinatorics of hook Kronecker coefficients may provide an alternate route to explicit interpretations or consistency checks.

other_useful_info: The paper repeatedly stresses that the main boson-fermion formula C_n(q;u;z)=E_n(q‚àíŒµu;z), Zabrocki's formula C_n(q+t;u;z)=sum_a u^a Œî'_{e_{n‚àía‚àí1}}e_n, and the two-fermion Theta formula are conjectural in the generality stated, although several specializations are known. The Rise version of the Delta Conjecture is known, while the Valley version remains open; the paper notes that the Valley version has a schedules formula and is naturally connected to the valley-decorated interpretation of hook components of ‚àá_v e_n. Equation 5.9 conjectures C_n(q+t;u+v;z)=sum_{k=0}^{n‚àí1} Œò_{e_k[(u+v)z]}(‚àáe_{n‚àík}), and the paper says only the q=t=0 specialization is known to match the Kim-Rhoades formula. Section 6 records a low-degree approximation C_n(q;0;z)=(n) h_n[Œ©(q)z]/h_n[Œ©(q)], meaning equality through total q-degree n; this could be useful for checking small hook shapes of bounded degree. Section 7 gives the explicit stable example E_3=1‚äós_3+(s_1+s_2)‚äós_21+(s_11+s_3)‚äós_111, hence C_3(q;0;z)=s_3(z)+(s_1(q)+s_2(q))s_21(z)+(s_11(q)+s_3(q))s_111(z) and C_3(0;u;z)=s_3(z)+(s_1(u)+s_11(u))s_21(z)+(s_2(u)+s_111(u))s_111(z); these are useful test cases for any proposed hook-coefficient interpretation. The paper also points out that the full-polynomial-ring coefficient r_Œº(q)=s_Œº[Œ©(q)] is tied to the classical restriction problem of decomposing GL_k characters when restricted to permutation matrices in S_k.
why_relevant: Develops conjectural and computational links between bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant modules and Theta/Macdonald operators. It contains many of the operator identities and special cases relevant to interpreting mixed bosonic-fermionic Hilbert and Frobenius series.
